1.matchText: the Manchu empire that had ruled China since 1644
1.blurb: The Qing dynasty, China's last imperial dynasty, collapsed in the 1911 Xinhai Revolution; the six-year-old Puyi abdicated in February 1912, ending two thousand years of imperial rule and creating the vacuum this book opens in.

Qin Shi Huang unified the warring states in 221 BCE and created the office of emperor and the centralized bureaucratic state that, in continuously evolving form, lasted until 1912 — the institution whose end this book begins with.

The Ottoman Empire collapsed at almost the same moment as the Qing and was rebuilt by the soldier Mustafa Kemal Atatürk into a secular single-party national republic — the same problem (a fallen multiethnic empire becoming a modern national state) that China took far longer and far more blood to solve.
The 'century of humiliation' narrative begins with the First Opium War and the unequal treaties the Qing signed from 1842, the grievance every later Chinese revolutionary, Mao above all, invoked.
The 1911 revolution is where the Qing story ended — the Xinhai collapse the revolutionary narrative grows directly out of.
China and the Ottomans both turned from millennia-old empires into struggling republics in the same generation — parallel imperial deaths.

3.blurb: India, decolonizing in the same decades, built a mass party (the Indian National Congress under Gandhi) that accepted elections and the peaceful transfer of power — the opposite of China's two armed Leninist parties, and the central contrast this book returns to.
May Fourth was the intellectual revolt Self-Strengthening had failed to become — the radical answer to the Qing's botched modernization.

5.blurb: Meiji Japan industrialized by state decree from the 1870s — railways, banks, a central bank, conscription — the model of top-down modernization the Nanjing Decade's defenders argue China was attempting before Japan invaded.

5.matchText: Japanese militarism built up in the Japan books
5.blurb: The 1931 Mukden Incident — the Kwantung Army staging a railway sabotage to seize Manchuria without Tokyo authorization — is narrated from the Japanese side in the opening chapter of the Japanese economic-miracle story, where the militarism the Meiji books built becomes the road to war with China and the United States.
The Nanjing Decade unfolded under the shadow of an imperial Japan that had already industrialized and was about to invade — Meiji's success as China's nemesis.

7.blurb: The Yellow River is called 'China's Sorrow' because it carries so much loess silt it changes course and floods catastrophically; the 1938 deliberate dike breach to slow Japan is one more chapter in a relationship with this river that runs through every Chinese book.

8.blurb: The 1943 Bengal Famine, in which British wartime policy and a stricken colonial administration let one to three million die in eastern India, ran in the same window as China's Henan famine — two of the deadliest famines of the war, both downstream of empire and war.

9.blurb: India's path to independence ended in a negotiated transfer of power and electoral politics rather than a fight to the death between armed parties — the divergence this book argues is the deepest difference between the Chinese and Indian answers to the same problem.

Koxinga, a Ming-loyalist warlord-pirate, expelled the Dutch from Taiwan in 1661 and made it a refuge from the mainland — the first time the island became a political haven against whoever ruled China, three centuries before Chiang Kai-shek did the same.
China's 1949 communist victory and India's 1947 independence-and-partition are the twin Asian decolonizations — opposite roads out of the colonial century.
The civil war resumed only because the Japanese empire Meiji built had finally been defeated — Chinese history hostage to the Japanese trajectory.

10.matchText: the Korean side of this story
10.blurb: The Korean War, which permanently divided the peninsula into two opposite states and locked China and the United States into two decades of hostility, is told from the Korean side in the modern Korea narrative.
'New China' was barely founded before the Korean War — the People's Republic blooded in its first year against the US across the Yalu.

12.matchText: as a functioning electoral democracy with a free press
12.blurb: India suffered serious food crises in the same era but, as an electoral democracy with a free press, never had a famine on the Great Leap's scale; by the 1970s the Green Revolution and a state that could be voted out had turned recurrent famine into a historical category — the sharpest test of the two roads.

13.matchText: a humiliation on the Indian side
13.blurb: The 1962 Sino-Indian border war was a short, sharp Chinese victory and a national humiliation in India that reshaped Nehru's last years and Indian defense policy, told from the Indian side in the Nehru's Republic chapter.
The Great Famine is Maoist development at its most catastrophic — the dark counter-case to India's slower, democratic post-colonial path.

14.blurb: While China lost the 1950s and 1960s to the Leap and the Cultural Revolution, Japan and the 'Asian Tigers' spent the same decades in record-breaking growth — the starkest single measure of what the Mao-era catastrophes cost China.
The Cultural Revolution is the chaos the next era — Deng's Rise of China — was a deliberate repudiation of.

15.matchText: foreign powers dictating terms to a prostrate Qing
15.blurb: The century that began with Britain dictating the Treaty of Nanking to a defeated Qing in 1842 ended, in a sense, with Nixon coming to Beijing in 1972 — China rejoining the great-power system on its own terms.

15.matchText: ran through every Chinese dynasty before it
15.blurb: The Mandate of Heaven — the idea that Heaven signals a dynasty's fall through disasters and rebellion — framed the legitimacy of every Chinese dynasty through the Qing; the 1976 Tangshan earthquake as Mao lay dying was read in exactly this ancient key.
